title: Antibiotic Stewardship: Teaching Appropriate Use and Preventing Resistance
subtitle: A 40-year-old with a 3-day viral cold asks for antibiotics. The nurse teaches that antibiotics don't treat viruses, explains antimicrobial resistance, provides symptom self-care, and reinforces infection prevention.

patient.name: Denise Carver
patient.admitDate: Primary-care clinic with 3 days of cough, congestion, and sore throat consistent with a viral upper respiratory infection. No signs of a bacterial infection. Requesting antibiotics; here for assessment and teaching.

vitals.temp: Cough, congestion, sore throat x3 days (viral)
No bacterial signs; viral URI likely
C. diff diarrhea / future resistant infection

nursesNote: Denise Carver, 40, with a 3-day viral upper respiratory infection requesting antibiotics. Antibiotics treat bacterial — not viral — infections; taking them for a cold is ineffective and contributes to antimicrobial resistance, a major public-health threat. Teaching: reserve antibiotics for bacterial infections and take them exactly as prescribed (complete the course, don't skip doses); never share or save leftover antibiotics; don't demand them for viral illness. Manage the cold with rest, fluids, and OTC symptom care. Prevent infections (and antibiotic need) with vaccines, hand hygiene, and food safety. Note antibiotics can disrupt normal flora and cause C. difficile diarrhea — report severe or bloody diarrhea. Provide return precautions.
providerOrders: Educate the patient that the illness is viral; antibiotics are not indicated
Teach symptom relief (rest, fluids, OTC care) and return precautions
Counsel on appropriate antibiotic use and antimicrobial resistance
Reinforce infection prevention: vaccines, hand hygiene, food safety
Teach not to share or save antibiotics; report severe/bloody diarrhea on antibiotics

opts.text: Antibiotics treat bacterial infections, not viral illnesses like colds or flu
Misusing or overusing antibiotics drives antimicrobial resistance
Antibiotics should be taken only when prescribed for a bacterial infection
Vaccines and hand hygiene help prevent infections and reduce antibiotic need
Antibiotics are an effective treatment for the common cold
Leftover antibiotics should not be saved or shared
Take the antibiotic exactly as prescribed and complete the course
Antibiotics disrupt normal flora and can cause C. difficile diarrhea
A viral illness is managed with rest, fluids, and symptom care
Demanding antibiotics for a viral illness is not appropriate
Sharing antibiotics with others is a safe way to treat similar symptoms
Report severe or bloody diarrhea while taking antibiotics

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the teaching for a viral illness

opts.text: Assess the illness and explain it is viral, so antibiotics won't help
Teach symptom relief: rest, fluids, and OTC care
Explain antibiotic resistance and appropriate use
Teach infection prevention (vaccines, hand hygiene) to reduce future need
Give return precautions and follow-up; document
Take antibiotics only for a confirmed or likely bacterial infection
Take the full course exactly as the provider prescribed
Prevent infections with vaccines and hand hygiene to reduce need
Use rest, fluids, and symptom care for a viral cold
Report severe or bloody diarrhea while on antibiotics
Demand antibiotics from the provider for a viral cold
Save leftover antibiotics for the next time you feel sick
Share antibiotics with a family member with similar symptoms
Stop the antibiotic early once you feel better

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Explain that the illness is viral and antibiotics are not indicated
Provide symptom-relief and self-care teaching
Counsel on appropriate antibiotic use and resistance
Reinforce prevention (vaccines, hand hygiene, food safety)
Provide return precautions and document the teaching
Antibiotics are reserved for bacterial infections
The patient finishes the prescribed course as directed
Vaccines and hand hygiene reduce infections and antibiotic need
A viral cold is managed with rest and fluids, no antibiotics
Antibiotics are taken for every cold "just in case"
Leftover antibiotics are shared and saved for later
Courses are stopped early or doses are skipped
A resistant infection develops after repeated unnecessary antibiotics

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Health Promotion and Maintenance, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
